original_abstract: Research interest in single-atom catalysts (SACs) has been continuously increasing. However, the lack of understanding of the dynamic behaviors of SACs during applications hinders catalyst development and mechanistic understanding. Herein, we report on the evolution of active sites over Pd/TiO2-anatase SAC (Pd1/TiO2) in the reverse water-gas shift (rWGS) reaction. Combining kinetics, in situ characterization, and theory, we show that at T ≥ 350 °C, the reduction of TiO2 by H2 alters the coordination environment of Pd, creating Pd sites with partially cleaved Pd-O interfacial bonds and a unique electronic structure that exhibit high intrinsic rWGS activity through the carboxyl pathway. The activation by H2 is accompanied by the partial sintering of single Pd atoms (Pd1) into disordered, flat, ∼1 nm diameter clusters (Pdn). The highly active Pd sites in the new coordination environment under H2 are eliminated by oxidation, which, when performed at a high temperature, also redisperses Pdn and facilitates the reduction of TiO2. In contrast, Pd1 sinters into crystalline, ∼5 nm particles (PdNP) during CO treatment, deactivating Pd1/TiO2. During the rWGS reaction, the two Pd evolution pathways coexist. The activation by H2 dominates, leading to the increasing rate with time-on-stream, and steady-state Pd active sites similar to the ones formed under H2. This work demonstrates how the coordination environment and nuclearity of metal sites on a SAC evolve during catalysis and pretreatments and how their activity is modulated by these behaviors. These insights on SAC dynamics and the structure-function relationship are valuable to mechanistic understanding and catalyst design.
humanized_abstract: Single-atom catalysts (SACs) have been extensively investigated. The underlying knowledge of the dynamic behavior of SACs during catalysis is unfortunately lacking. Here, the evolution of active sites over a Pd/TiO2-anatase SAC, denoted as Pd1/TiO2 (Pd1: single Pd atom) during reverse water-gas shift (rWGS) is investigated. The influence of kinetic parameters and in situ observations along with theoretical calculations reveal that at temperatures (T) above 350 °C, the rWGS over Pd1/TiO2 is catalyzed by hydrogen (H2) activated Pd sites with partially cleaved Pd-O interfacial bonds that exhibit unique electronic characteristics resulting in high intrinsic rWGS activity via carboxyl pathway. The observation of activation of Pd1 sites by H2 and subsequent partial sintering into disordered flat clusters of diameter around 1 nm (denoted as Pdn) is reported. These highly active Pd sites with the new coordination environment are readily inactivated by oxidation. When oxidation is carried out at higher temperatures, Pdn clusters are redispersed and the TiO2 is fully reduced. In CO, Pd1 sites sinter into large crystalline particles of diameter around 5 nm (denoted as PdNP). During rWGS over Pd1/TiO2, the two pathways of Pd evolution are observed, where the activation of Pd1 sites by H2 dominates and is responsible for the increasing rate with time-on-stream and the presence of steady state Pd active sites that are similar to those formed under H2. This study for the first time demonstrates the coordination environment and nuclearity of the metal sites on the surface of a SAC can evolve under different catalytic conditions and pretreatments and how these evolutions modulate the activity of the resulting metal sites. This work not only sheds light on the dynamic behavior of SACs during catalysis, but also provides fundamental understanding of the structure-function relationship over SACs.
